coredata.dc:title: Sugar moiety driven adsorption of nucleic acid on graphene quantum dots: Photophysical, thermodynamic and theoretical evidence
coredata.dc:creator: Maiti, Susmita
Karmakar, Sudip
Kundu, Somashree
Nath Roy, Chandra
Kanti Das, Tushar
Chakraborty, Brahmananda
Saha, Abhijit
coredata.dc:description: Integration of biologically important molecules with graphene quantum dots (GQDs) as a new class of luminescent water-soluble carbon materials, has drawn considerable attention for recent exciting progress on its biomedical applications. In order to avoid toxic semiconductor QDs, GQDs have shown great promise in detection of DNA and other biological applications and hence, fundamental understanding of processes of interactions is important and necessary. The present work establishes the significant role of sugar residue of nucleic acids in adsorption of graphene materials through experimental estimation of binding thermodynamics concomitant with density functional theoretical (DFT) simulation and a detailed evaluation of photophysical parameters. Maximum quenching efficiency of adenosine moiety coupled with the shift in the vibrations of the five-member ring of D-ribose demonstrates the adsorption of DNA on GQDs framework through sugar moieties. The observed experimental results are consistent with the favorable Gibb’s free energy calculated through theoretical simulation. DFT presenting orbital interactions and bonding mechanism of DNAs on GQDs confirms that adsorption on GQDs surface involves charge transfer from nucleobases to GQDs and adenosine, in particular, binds strongly through charge transfer of 0.032e from nitrogen atom to carbon of GQDs. The present investigation will provide insights in understanding the possible role of GQDs in cellular imaging, biosensing and as carriers in targeted drug delivery.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
DNA
Isothermal titration calorimetry
DFT
Charge transfer

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Geometry optimization and computation of Gibb’s free energy
Charge transfer & spatial distribution charge density

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Department of Science and Technology, Ministry of Science and Technology, India
Council of Scientific and Industrial Research, India

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Authors (S. Maiti and S. Karmakar) are indebted to Department of Science & Technology, Govt. of India, for the INSPIRE Fellowship. S. Kundu and T. K. Das are gratified to the University Grants Commission and CSIR for the NET fellowship. BC would like to thank Dr. Nandini Garg, Dr. T. Sakuntala, Dr. S.M. Yusuf and Dr. A. K. Mohanty for support and encouragement. BC also thanks the staff of BARC computer division for supercomputing facility.

Authors (S. Maiti and S. Karmakar) are indebted to Department of Science & Technology, Govt. of India, for the INSPIRE Fellowship. S. Kundu and T. K. Das are gratified to the University Grants Commission and CSIR for the NET fellowship. BC would like to thank Dr. Nandini Garg, Dr. T. Sakuntala, Dr. S.M. Yusuf and Dr. A. K. Mohanty for support and encouragement. BC also thanks the staff of BARC computer division for supercomputing facility.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Absorption spectra of GQDs, adenine and adenine-GQDs mixture; Inset:Optical image of GQDs under UV light.
Changes in fluorescence spectra of GQDs at different concentrations of adenosine in 10 mM phosphate buffer, pH 7.4, Inset: Linear Stern-Volmer plot on fluorescence quenching.
FTIR spectra of (a) blank adenine, blank GQDs and adenine-GQDs mixture, (b) blank adenosine, blank GQDs and adenosine-GQDs mixture.
Isothermal titration calorimetric results on interactions of GQDs with adenine (a), thymine (b), cytosine (c), poly A (d), ATP (e) and ssDNA (f).

DFT relaxed structure of (a) Adenine, (b) Adenosine and (c) ATP adsorbed on GQDs; Orange, Blue, Black, Violet and Red represents Oxygen, Phosphorus, Carbon, Nitrogen and hydrogen, respectively. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Total Density of States for (a) GQDs, (b) ATP adsorbed on GQDs; EF is at 0 eV and shown with dotted black line.

PDOS for 2p states of nitrogen atom of Adenosine for isolated Adenosine (left panel upper side), when attached on Graphene quantum Dots (lower left panel); PDOS for 2p states of carbon atom of GQDs for perfect case (upper right panel) and when bonded with Adenosine (lower right panel); EF is at 0 eV and shown with dotted line.

Charge density Difference plot, ρ(GQDs + ATP) - ρ(GQDs) for is o-value of 0.07819e. Red and blue color denotes charge loss and charge gain area respectively. It clearly shows that ATP losses charge. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Fluorescence decay parameter of GQDs in the presence of different concentrations of adenosine solution.
Thermodynamic parameters determined from calorimetric measurements of interaction of DNA and its component towards GQDs.
Thermodynamic parameters determined from temperature dependent calorimetric measurements on interactions of ssDNA towards GQDs.
Comparison of Gibb’s free energy change between Theory and Experiment.

head.ce:title: Sugar moiety driven adsorption of nucleic acid on graphene quantum dots: Photophysical, thermodynamic and theoretical evidence

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: UGC-DAE Consortium for Scientific Research, Kolkata Centre, III/LB-8 Bidhannagar, Kolkata 700106, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: UGC-DAE Consortium for Scientific Research, Kolkata Centre, III/LB-8 Bidhannagar, Kolkata 700106, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: High Pressure and Synchrotron Radiation Physics Division, Bhabha Atomic Research Centre, Trombay, Mumbai 40085, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: High Pressure and Synchrotron Radiation Physics Division

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: High Pressure and Synchrotron Radiation Physics Division, Bhabha Atomic Research Centre, Trombay, Mumbai 40085, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Homi Bhabha National Institute, Mumbai 40094, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Homi Bhabha National Institute, Mumbai 40094, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Integration of biologically important molecules with graphene quantum dots (GQDs) as a new class of luminescent water-soluble carbon materials, has drawn considerable attention for recent exciting progress on its biomedical applications. In order to avoid toxic semiconductor QDs, GQDs have shown great promise in detection of DNA and other biological applications and hence, fundamental understanding of processes of interactions is important and necessary. The present work establishes the significant role of sugar residue of nucleic acids in adsorption of graphene materials through experimental estimation of binding thermodynamics concomitant with density functional theoretical (DFT) simulation and a detailed evaluation of photophysical parameters. Maximum quenching efficiency of adenosine moiety coupled with the shift in the vibrations of the five-member ring of D-ribose demonstrates the adsorption of DNA on GQDs framework through sugar moieties. The observed experimental results are consistent with the favorable Gibb’s free energy calculated through theoretical simulation. DFT presenting orbital interactions and bonding mechanism of DNAs on GQDs confirms that adsorption on GQDs surface involves charge transfer from nucleobases to GQDs and adenosine, in particular, binds strongly through charge transfer of 0.032e from nitrogen atom to carbon of GQDs. The present investigation will provide insights in understanding the possible role of GQDs in cellular imaging, biosensing and as carriers in targeted drug delivery.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: The interactions between nano-structured and biomolecules have been attracted tremendous attention in molecular biotechnology, biomedical imaging, material sciences, and so forth [1–7]. Among various types of nanomaterials, carbonaceous nanostructures like graphene, water-soluble C60 derivatives, carbon nanotubes, etc. have been extensively employed in drug delivery, bioimaging, gene therapy and biosensing [8–16]. Now-a-days as a brilliant member of graphene family, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have generated considerable interest in different research arena, like photo-catalysis, drug delivery, biomedicine, biosensing, bioimaging, etc. [17–21]. Further, there are various recent developments on the practical use of GQDs in technologically important fields, especially in the area of solar hydrogen production, fabricating supercapacitor etc. [22–24] Unlike other family members, such as carbon dots (CDs), carbon nanotubes, etc., GQD clearly has significant advantages as it possess a graphene structure inside the dots. Most importantly, exceptional biocompatibility, extended π − π conjugated network, high luminescence quantum efficiency and good water solubility make GQDs more attractive compared to the traditional semiconductor quantum dots.

Recently, considerable work has been pursued to follow interactions of protein and DNA with graphene oxide or reduced graphene oxide. Interaction study of DNA with nanoparticles has generated significant interest in various applications because of having reproducibility, programmability, and specificity. Varghese et al specified isothermal titration calorimetry as a tool for investigating the interactions of graphene oxide materials with DNA nucleobases and nucleosides [25]. The experimental results presented a trend of relative binding strength in the order of guanine (G) > adenine (A) > cytosine (C)∼thymine (T) [25]. On contrary, calculation based on ab-initio density functional theory (DFT) showed slightly reverse trend like C > G > A > T [26]. Still there is no explanation available for this discrepancy. The adsorption of single-stranded DNA (ssDNA) and RNA can preferentially occur on surface of graphene by π-π stacking interactions between the carbon rings of the graphene and ring atoms of the nucleobases which are confirmed by both experimental and theoretical results [8]. In contrast, there is lower affinity of double-stranded DNA towards graphene [27–29]. Qian et al demonstrated DNA detection by fluorescence sensing approach based on fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) involving graphene oxide and graphene quantum dots [30]. In another work, Kermani et al reported a new method for fluorescence based assay and anisotropic investigation of DNA methyltransferase enzyme activity founded on DNA modified GQDs [31]. Wang et al designed GQDs-aptamer (cDNA) bioconjugates as fluorescent probe, for high-sensitivity detection of ochratoxin A (OTA), a type of mycotoxin present in variety of foods [32]. Thus, fundamental understanding of the binding with various biological components and its influence on photophysical properties of GQDs is important and necessary. Quantum simulations can provide the theoretical insight for the bonding and orbital interactions of DNAs with graphene quantum dots. This has motivated our interest to explore the interactions of graphene quantum dots with biological important DNA and its constituent nucleobases, nucleosides, nucleotides in terms of thermodynamical and photophysical characteristics as well electronic properties employing Density Functional Theory (DFT) simulations.

Isothermal titration calorimetry (ITC) helps in evaluating the thermodynamic as well as kinetic aspects from two interacting aqueous solutions when one is titrated against the other. It is often used for studying the thermodynamics between biological systems. ITC has been applied in investigating protein-DNA interactions [33], protein-lipid interactions [34], protein–protein interactions, protein-GQDs interactions [35], amino acid-GO interactions [36], GO-DNA interactions [25], metal-DNA bases interactions [37], etc. Here, the energetics of interaction of DNA and its components with GQDs are observed by ITC. Herein, binding of DNA and its components with GQDs has been examined by isothermal titration calorimetry. There is a linear correlation of enthalpy − entropy compensation analysis between the thermodynamic parameters which is exactly similar to protein-small molecular interaction. Further, we have endeavored to understand interactions between DNA and its components towards GQDs through UV–vis absorption, photoluminescence and FTIR spectroscopic techniques. The results observed in the study indicate that the binding affinity of DNA and its component towards GQDs is different from those obtained with GO. Further, the present study presents a comparison of all thermodynamic parameters in cases of nucleobases, nucleosides and also quantitative estimation involved in binding processes, which have not been reported earlier in case of carbon nanomaterials. Experimental findings were qualitatively supported by theoretical data from state-of-the-art DFT simulations.

Natural graphite powder (300 mesh) was purchased from Alfa Aesar and was used without further purification. Perchloric acid (70 % v/v), sodium hydroxide, hydrochloric acid were obtained from Merck, India. Single-stranded DNA (ssDNA) from calf thymus, poly adenylic acid potassium salt (poly A), poly cytidylic acid potassium salt (poly C), polyguanylic acid potassium salt (poly G) were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich, Germany. DNA nucleobases e.g. adenine (A), thymine (T), cytosine (C), guanine (G), nucleosides (adenosine, thymidine, guanosine and cytidine), adenosine-5́-monophophate (AMP), adenosine-5́-triphosphte (ATP), double-stranded DNA (ds DNA) were purchased from SRL chemicals, India. All experiments were carried out in 10 mM phosphate buffer at pH 7.4. All chemicals used were of analytical reagent grade and Milli-Q water was used in the experiment. Concentration of the stock DNA solution was assessed by measuring absorbance at 260 nm with extinction coefficients (ε) for double stranded DNA is 6600 M−1 cm−1 and for single stranded DNA is 10000 M−1 cm−1, respectively.[38] Concentration of poly(A) in terms of nucleotide was estimated taking molar absorption coefficient (ε) value of 10,000 M−1 cm−1 [39,40].

At first, by using Hummer’s method, graphite oxide was synthesized by oxidizing graphite with H2SO4 (98 % v/v), KMnO4 and NaNO2 [41]. Then to prepare graphene oxide (GO), graphite oxide thus obtained was further exfoliated via sonication. The synthesis and characterization of GO were described in detail in our previous work [42]. GQDs was synthesized by using the procedure reported by us earlier [43]. In brief, as-synthesized GO solution (10 mL) taken in a round bottle flask was mixed with concentrated perchloric acid (HClO4) and stirred for 30 min at room temperature. The mixed solution was refluxed for 24 h and the cooled solution was subsequently sonicated for 1 h. Then, on neutralizing this solution to pH 7.0 by adding sodium hydroxide solution, the color turned to deep brown denoting the formation of GQDs.

The interactions of DNA and its component with GQDs were observed through absorption spectral measurement using Perkin Elmer (UV/VIS/NIR Lambda 950) spectrophotometer. Photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopic measurement was performed with a Perkin Elmer (LS 55) luminescence spectrometer at room temperature.

Infra-red spectral measurements of GQDs, DNA components and their mixture were carried out with FTIR spectrometer (Perkin Elmer, Spectrum GX).Samples before measurements were made by casting a few drops of solution of respective component on the CaF2 window, which were dried under vacuum before measurement. The spectra were acquired in transmission mode with an average of 20 scans at a resolution of 4 cm−1. Sonication was performed with Ultrasonic Homogenizer (Model: U650) Takashi, Japan at 25 kHz using 2 mm probe.

Fluorescence lifetime measurements of GQDs were performed by a time-correlated single-photon-counting (TCSPC) spectrometer (Horiba Jobin Yovon) using nanosecond diode LED of 280 nm (Horiba scientific, DD-280) and the decay kinetics was recorded at 437 nm. The data analysis was performed using DAS-6 decay analysis software. The fluorescence decay was analyzed by employing multi-exponential fitting program considering reduced chi-square value.

Isothermal titration calorimetry experiment has been carried out by using calorimeter (ITC-200, Microcal, Northampton, MA) to know the heat changes of the interactions between GQDs and DNA components at constant temperature 30 °C, in 10 mM phosphate buffer, pH 7.4. GQDs solution (1 mg/mL) and 10 mM DNA components was taken in cell and syringe, respectively. During titration, 2 μL of DNA components were injected 19 times in sequence to 200 µL of GQDs solution. The heat of dilution of DNA components was measured in each run, using the same experimental parameters by adding buffer solution without GQDs. Heat of dilution was deducted from the observed heat released in particular titration experiment and the final results were fitted by the ORIGIN software (Microcal ITC200, Northampton, MA).

Zeta potential was measured using light scattering spectrometer (DLS-nano ZS, Zetasizer, Nanoseries, Malvern Instruments) at 25 0C. Samples were filtered several times through 0.22 mm Millipore membrane prior to each measurement. The zeta potential was estimated adopting Smoluchowski equation in the commercial software and from the electrophoretic mobility.

Absorption spectra of blank GQDs, blank adenine and adenine-GQDs mixture are shown in Fig. 1 . Two absorption bands at 270 nm and 317 nm may be attributed to n - π* and π - π* transitions of CO and CC of bonds of the oxidized aromatic structure of GQDs, respectively [44]. It is known that spectral changes occur due to the changes in electronic structure/distribution of the interacting components and there can be red shift due to binding of aromatic groups in nucleic acid [45]. Here, thus red shift of absorption of adenine (264 nm to 278 nm) and spectral broadening suggests possible ground state binding between adenine and GQDs. We have also studied the absorption behavior of other nucleobases, nucleosides, nucleotides and ss-DNA on interactions with GQDs. To illustrate, changes in absorption spectra of GQDs in the presence of adenosine are also shown in Fig S3. In case of ss-DNA, there is no significant shift in the absorption spectra of DNA in the presence of GQDs and this suggests weak interactions between ss-DNA and GQDs.

Fluorescence spectroscopy is a very sensitive technique since the relaxation time of excited state is of a few nanosecond which is similar to the time domain of many important biological processes, such as the rotational motion of molecules, molecular binding, and conformational changes. Fig. 2 shows fluorescence quenching of GQDs on excitation with 280 nm photons when concentration of adenosine was enhanced from 0 to 0.44 mM. This fluorescence quenching follows a linear Stern–Volmer relationship (eqn. (1)) [46] in the presence of increasing concentration of adenosine (Fig. 2: inset),
(1)
I 0
I
= 1 +
K
SV
Q
where I0, I are the fluorescence intensities of GQDs in the absence and in the presence of adenosine, respectively, KSV, Stern-Volmer constant and [Q], the concentration of quencher. The value of Stern-Volmer constant is
5.9 ×
10
3
M−1. Further, with increasing concentrations of adenosine, a peak appearing around 367 nm (Fig. S4) presumably may be due to the formation of exiplex of GQDs with adenosine. Adenosine is expected to adsorb on the surface of GQDs through hydrophobic π-π interaction. Additionally, the oxygenated functional groups lying at the edges and the bases of the GQDs surface and adenosine may interact through charge transfer process. We have also studied fluorescence quenching of GQDs in the presence of adenine, ATP and ss-DNA. The Stern-Volmer constants have been estimated through linear fitting of the eqn.1 and these are summarized in Table S1. The quenching efficiencies with regard to DNA components follow the order: adenosine > adenine > ATP > ss-DNA. The observed results suggest that nucleosides interact more strongly than the other DNA components due to additional interaction between the functional group of GQDs and the sugar moiety of nucleoside. According to the earlier report DNA and its component can attach in a planar orientation of GO [7]. Similarly, here hydrophobic π-stacking interaction between basal planes of the ending nucleotides and the graphitic surface may facilitate attachment of DNA and its components onto the surface of GQDs and this may lead to quenching of the fluorescence of GQDs [47]. Additionally, interactions between the oxygenated functional groups lying at the edges and the bases of the GQDs surface and nucleobases of DNA may get strengthened through H-bond and this is similar to the earlier report on interactions of GO with DNA [25].

The binding of DNA and its component towards GQDs has been further monitored through FTIR spectroscopy. Fig. 3 demonstrates the FTIR spectra of adenine, adenosine and GQDs in its native form and in the presence of GQDs. On closer look, it is observed that band at 536 cm−1 assigned to out-of-plane vibrations of the amino group of adenine gets shifted to 575 cm−1 on binding with GQDs. Further, the observed shift of bands at 1053, 1594 cm−1 assigned to C8-N9 and N3-C4 stretching modes of adenine [48] to 1064, 1588 cm−1, respectively, could be due to binding of these functional groups to GQDs surface. Such types of binding are likely to influence vibrations of the five member ring in adenine. The FTIR spectrum of adenosine-GQDs mixture shows strong sharp peaks at 853, 983, 1087, 1646 and 3350 cm−1 which were attributed to out of plane vibration of υ(NH) of adenine, bending mode of (OCH) of D-ribose, bending mode of ribose (COH), stretching vibration of CO and stretching mode of (OH) from D-ribose [49,50]. All peaks get shifted from the blank adenosine due to the binding between adenosine and GQDs. The shift in the vibrations of the five member ring of D-ribose suggests the bonding between GQDs and sugar moiety of nucleoside.

Fig. S5(a) shows that ATP is likely to bind on the surface of GQDs through pendant NH2 of adenine moiety. The band appears at 1666 cm−1 for ATP-GQDs mixture could be attributed to υ(NH) deformation of exocyclic—NH2. It appears from the spectra of ATP-GQDs mixture (Fig. S5(a)) that bands at 934 cm−1 and 1086 cm−1 may be assigned to PO bond vibration of ATP [51]. Hence, it may be inferred that phosphate groups with negative charge facilitate the attachment of ATP on surface of GQDs.

FTIR spectrum of blank ss-DNA (Fig. S5(b)) shows several in-plane vibrations, such as 1717 cm−1 (G and T), 1663 cm−1 (T, G, A and C), 1609 cm−1 (A and C), 1492 cm−1 (C and G) and 1222 cm−1 (PO2 asymmetric stretch) [52]. These bands get shifted correspondingly to 1705 cm−1, 1653 cm−1, 1486 cm−1 and 1230 cm−1 due to the bonding between ss-DNA and GQDs. The band at 3358 cm−1 owing to –NH2 vibration gets superimposed over the DNA-GQDs mixture resulting in the aforementioned broadening.

Time resolved fluorescence spectroscopy has been performed to understand the quenching process of the DNA and its component towards the emission of GQDs. Fig. S6 demonstrates time resolved emission decay profile of blank GQDs and in the presence of increasing concentration of adenosine. The relaxation data were fitted in the tri-exponential relation (eqn. (2)),
(2)
f
t
=
∑
i = 1
3
A i
e
- t /
τ i
where τi is the lifetime of different components and the pre-exponential factor; Ai, provides an estimate of the contribution of each component to the total decay. The average fluorescence lifetime, τav is determined with the help of the following relation (eqn. (3)) [53].
(3)
τ
av
=
∑
i = 1
3
A i
τ
i
2
/
∑
i = 1
3
A i
τ i

The average lifetime of blank GQDs is 10.3 ns that supports our earlier report [43]. Table 1 shows lifetimes components (τ1, τ2 and τ3) with corresponding amplitudes (A1, A2 and A3), and average lifetime of GQDs emission (τav) with increasing concentration of adenosine. The average lifetime of GQDs decreases from 10.3 ns to 6.4 ns with increasing concentration of adenosine indicates the fluorescence of GQDs can be quenched in the presence of adenosine through dynamic quenching method. There is decrease in all lifetime components to the maximum extent of about 67 % and this presumably suggests a strong interactive nature/play between GQDs and nucleotide. Thus, it is clear that lifetime components of GQDs are affected by the presence of nucleotide.

Isothermal titration calorimetry is extensively used to investigate biomolecular interactions in quantitative terms [54] and it directly measures the heat of reaction when the reaction is going on. Free energy (ΔG) and entropy (ΔS) were determined from the measured values of binding constant (K) and enthalpy (ΔH), using the standard thermodynamic equations, ΔG = − RT ln K and ΔG = ΔH – TΔS, In order to explore the possible interactions between GQDs and DNA components through calorimetric measurements, different sets of DNA components, like nucleobases (A, T, G, C), nucleosides, nucleotides (AMP, ATP), poly A, poly G and ssDNA were chosen in the present investigation.

A closer look on the experimental results reveals that binding of GQDs with DNA and its components are favored by enthalpy change (ΔH < 0)with entropy change (ΔS > 0) for nucleobases except adenine. According to the earlier literature, the conditions: ΔH < 0, ΔS > 0; ΔH < 0, ΔS < 0 and ΔH > 0, ΔS > 0, suggest that the primary force of interactions are electrostatic, Vander Waals force or hydrogen bond and hydrophobic in nature, respectively [56]. So, the results indicate that electrostatic and π-π interactions are the main binding force in case of T and C and Van der Waals force or hydrogen bond formation is the main binding force in case of adenine.

Further, from the thermodynamic parameters derived from the micro-calorimetric measurements, it is observed that favorable free energy change (-ΔG) in the interactions of thymine (T) or cytosine (C) with GQDs is the result of negative enthalpy (ΔH) change supplemented with positive entropy (ΔS) change. In case of nucleobases A, T and C, it appears that ΔH contributes to the free energy ΔG to the greater extent than that by TΔS. Hence, it may be inferred that binding of nucleobases with graphene quantum dots (GQDs) is an enthalpy driven process.

In case of nucleosides ΔH values are higher than other DNA components because of the presence of the exocyclic amino group in the structures. Though there are similarity in structures of adenosine and guanosine, but they are energetically very different. The trend in the relative interaction energies in terms of enthalpy change is (adenosine > thymidine > guanosine > cytidine), is same as that of nucleobases. Also the binding parameters reveal that the contribution of ΔH to the free energy ΔG is larger than that of TΔS. So, the interaction between nucleosides and GQDs are enthalpy driven process.

In case of nucleotides adenosine mono phosphate (AMP) binds more strongly than adenosine tri phosphate (ATP). The binding parameters (Table 2) suggest interaction between nucleotides and GQDs are enthalpy driven process. Calorimetric measurements reveal weak interaction of polyadenylic acid (poly A) with GQDs, but neither with polyguanylic acid (poly G) nor with poly cytidylic acid (poly C). The binding constant for poly A to GQDs is in the order of 104 M−1. ssDNA binds more strongly than dsDNA, and the binding constant for ssDNA with GQDs is in the order of 105 M−1 range. This may be attributed to the dominant contribution of electrostatic and π − π interactions in GQDs − DNA interactions through oxygen-rich groups and aromatic backbone of GQDs, respectively. Interactions of poly A and ss-DNA towards GQDs are enthalpy driven process, which suggests non-covalent complexation.

Among the all DNA components and ssDNA, adenosine exhibits strongest interaction (ΔH = 25.6 kcal mol−1 ) towards GQDs. It may reasonably be assumed that purine moiety of adenosine having extended aromatic surface and highest polarizability in comparison with other DNA components facilitate such strong interactions with GQDs [25]. The smaller ΔH values in the cases of nucleobases, nucleotides, in comparison with nucleosides, along with lesser fluorescence quenching ability signify weak interaction with GQDs. According to the earlier literature reports, though DNA and GO both are negative in charge, DNA can even be adsorbed on the GO surface. The π − π stacking and hydrophobic forces may be responsible for this particular adsorption [57,58]. Due to possessing large extended aromatic surface, π − π interaction becomes the key factor in the interactions of nucleosides with GQDs, and consequently, the large change in enthalpy (ΔH) plays the driving role in adsorption process. Further also the very binding between DNA and GQDs complex helps DNA for being good candidate for designing sensors for proteins, DNA/RNA, ions and small molecules.

Similar to several host − guest interactions, opposite trend towards enthalpy and entropy change involved in case of interactions of GQDs with nucleobases and nucleosides put forward evidence of possibility of enthalpy − entropy compensation [59]. In order to understand the effect, relation between TΔS and ΔH is expressed in terms of linear relation (eqn.4). By introducing this eqn. (4) into the Gibbs–Helmholtz equation followed by the differential, eqn. (5) is derived.
(4)
T Δ S = α Δ H + T Δ
S 0
(5)
δ Δ G =
1 - α
δ Δ H

In support of calorimetric observations, relatively stronger interactions of GQDs with nucleosides or nucleotides in comparison with nucleobases can also be confirmed from the consideration of overall charge of the components of DNA. Thus, the zeta potential of native GQDs, DNA components and their complexes were measured. The blank GQDs show zeta potential −35.5 mV, which decreases to −27.1, −17.9 and −21.1 mV, respectively due to the complexation of GQDs with adenine, adenosine and ATP. This suggests that ionic groups, like COO– and O– lying on the planes and edges of GQDs permit electrostatic interactions with nucleosides or nucleotides, and the aromatic segment on the surface favors π–π stacking interaction [63].

We have carried out quantum simulations using the plane wave Density Functional Theory code VASP [64–67]. We have employed PBE-GGA [68] exchange–correlation functional. The Cut-off energy for the plane wave was considered as 500 eV. The structural relaxation was carried out till the forces on each atom become<0.01 eV/Ǻ and the convergence criteria for the energy during electronic iteration was taken as 10-6 eV. To describe the long range Van der waals forces Grimme DFT-D2 [69] dispersion correction was incorporated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Geometry optimization and computation of Gibb’s free energy
ce:section.ce:para: Theoretical calculations are performed mainly to account for the interactions of 3 DNA components, e.g, Adenine from Nucleobase, Adenosine from Nucleoside and ATP from Nucleotide on GQDs. First, we have optimized the structure of GQDs as displayed in Fig. S-7(a). To optimize the individual DNAs, we have placed the molecules Adenine, ATP and Adenosine at the middle of the simulation cell having dimension of 30 Å × 30 Å × 30 Å. Sufficient vacuum makes the interactions between the periodic images negligible. The DFT optimized structure of Adenine, ATP and Adenosine are depicted in Fig. S-7(b,c,d). To study the interactions of DNAs on GQDs surface, we have placed Adenine, ATP and Adenosine at a height of 3 Å from GQD surface and performed the structural relaxations. The relaxed structure for Adenine, Adenosine and ATP adsorbed on GQDs surface are displayed in Fig. 5. Adenine, ATP and Adenosine are adsorbed on GQDs with favorable change in Gibb’s free energy as tabulated in Table 4 along with the experimental data. We can notice from Table 4 that Adenosine is bonded more strongly, followed by ATP and the bonding of Adenine is relatively weak. Interestingly, the trend in Gibb’s free energy change support experimental observation qualitatively. In the relaxed structure, O of Adenosine forms bond with carbon atom of GQDs having bond distance of 2.92 Å whereas nitrogen atom of ATP bonded on GQDs surface with CN bond length of 3.14 Å. Fig. 6 depicts total electronic Density of States of GQDs and ATP adsorbed on GQDs. We can observe an increase in quantum states close to Fermi level when ATP is adsorbed on GQDs. The enhancement in states may be due to adsorption of ATP. It may be mentioned here that on computing the change in Gibb’s free energy, we have not considered the solvent medium, where it may be little bit different. The present theoretical calculations are considered at vacuum conditions.

To get some theoretical insight on the interactions mechanism, Partial Density of States (PDOS) analysis and Bader charge transfer have been carried out. As Adenosine is bonded more strongly, PDOS and charge transfer are investigated only for Adenosine absorbed on GQDs. Fig. 7 shows the PDOS for 2pstates of N atom of Adenosine for isolated Adenosine (upper left panel) and when adsorbed on GQDs (lower left panel). To get comparison, PDOS for 2p orbital of carbon atom of Graphene quantum Dots for pristine case (upper right panel) and when bonded with Adenosine (lower right panel) were also displayed. X axis has been shifted in such a way that The Fermi level (EF) is located 0 eV and denoted with dotted line. We can clearly see (from right panel of Fig. 7) that there is an increase in intensity of electronic states close to EFfor carbon 2p states of GQDs when it interacts with Adenosine compared to pristine case. Also for 2p orbital of N atom of Adenosine there is depletion of states when it is adsorbed on GQDs compared to isolated Adenosine. The augmentation of states for 2p states of carbon atoms of GQDs and depletion of states for N 2p orbital of Adenosine signifies the charge transfer from Adenosine to GQDs. So the bonding is mainly due to interaction of 2p orbital of N of Adenosine and 2p orbital of C of GQDs. So, this π − π stacking interactions is responsible for adsorption of DNAs on GQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Charge transfer & spatial distribution charge density
ce:section.ce:para: To get the quantitative picture of electron transfer, Bader charge analysis [70] have been carried out. As per Bader charge portioning, there is transfer of 0.032e charge from 2p states of nitrogen of Adenosine to 2p orbital of C of GQDs. So the charge transfer is consistent with the PDOS analysis. For ATP, the charge transfer is 0.0303e as presented in Table-S2, less than that of Adenosine. Higher charge transfer and lower bond length justifies the stronger adsorption of Adenosine compared to ATP. Fig. 8 shows the visual display of the spatial distribution for the difference in electron density between GQDs + ATP and GQD, i.e, ρ(GQDs + ATP) - ρ(GQDs) with is o-value of 0.07819e. The charge loss is denoted by red region whereas the charge gain is denoted by blue region. The spatial distribution of charge density difference clearly indicates charge loss from ATP.

Spectroscopic approach has been systematically employed to investigate the possible interactions of DNA and its components to graphene quantum dots. Quenching analysis suggests possibility of the greatest interaction of GQDs with adenosine, followed by adenine, ATP, and ssDNA and this trend is very similar to that observed from the calorimetric measurements. We have also estimated the thermodynamic properties for interaction of GQDs with DNA and its component by using ITC. It has been demonstrated that electrostatic and π − π interactions contributes predominantly in the interaction processed involved. Calorimetric data also reveal that adenosine exhibits strongest interaction (ΔH = 25.6 kcal mol−1 ) towards GQDs compared to the other DNA components. The trend in the relative interaction energies in terms of enthalpy change for nucleosides is (adenosine > thymidine > guanosine > cytidine), is same as that of nucleobases to GQDs. The ITC results suggest that the π − π interaction is the key factor and as a result, the enthalpy change (ΔH) directs the complexation process. The enthalpy entropy compensation plot shows an excellent linear relationship with near unit slope which is similar to the several other host − guest systems. These results strongly indicate the applicability of GQDs as a biological receptor. From DFT simulations, we can predict that the adsorption of DNAs on GQDs are due to π − π interactions where there is electron transport from 2p states of DNA to 2p states of GQDs. DNAs have favorable adsorption energy and computed Gibb’s free energy changes are in the same trend as observed from experiments. These kinds of strategic studies involving both experimental and theoretical approaches can provide useful information in understanding the biological effects of GQDs in different applications like, cell imaging, drug delivery, and biosensing in near future.

Susmita Maiti: Methodology, Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation. Sudip Karmakar: Data curation, Formal analysis, Visualization. Somashree Kundu: Methodology, Data curation. Chandra Nath Roy: Visualization, Methodology. Tushar Kanti Das: Data curation, Methodology. Brahmananda Chakraborty: Formal analysis, Software, Validation, Writing - review & editing. Abhijit Saha: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Authors (S. Maiti and S. Karmakar) are indebted to Department of Science & Technology, Govt. of India, for the INSPIRE Fellowship. S. Kundu and T. K. Das are gratified to the University Grants Commission and CSIR for the NET fellowship. BC would like to thank Dr. Nandini Garg, Dr. T. Sakuntala, Dr. S.M. Yusuf and Dr. A. K. Mohanty for support and encouragement. BC also thanks the staff of BARC computer division for supercomputing facility.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.molliq.2022.121148.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
